t: Gradual discontinuation (taper) is the gold standard:
conversion to equivalent dose of long-acting benzodiazepine (diazepam), then reduction by 10–25% per week; total 6–12 weeks (sometimes months). Rapid discontinuation life-threatening.
Bedwetting — severe disruption or comorbid substance use.
Psychosocial intervention — CBT for anxiety or sleep disorder; this reduces need for pharmacotherapy.
Prescription management — at the primary healthcare level.
Ashton Manual — practical clinical guide by Ashton C.H. Heather (1999).
Conversion to diazepam equivalent, then gradual reduction; family support.
— Alternative to benzodiazepine use; CBT-I for sleep.
Gradual taper and significant improvement with CBT.
In older adults, discontinuation is both medically and psychologically difficult.
Myth 1: "Benzodiazepines can be taken safely for life"
long-term use — tolerance, dependence, cognitive impairment, fall risk (in elderly). NICE — short-term use.
Rapid discontinuation seizure and death risk; gradual taper critical.
Z-drugs also cause dependence; AAFP and NICE recommend short-term use.
FDA black box warning — this combination significantly increases risk of respiratory depression and death.
Following the abortion, alternative treatment (CBT, anxiety management) is necessary.
WHO. ICD-11. 6C43 Disorders due to sedative, hypnotic, anxiolytic. 2024.
Ashton C.H. Benzodiazepines: How They Work and How to Withdraw (Ashton Manual). 2002.
FDA Drug Safety Communication. Opioid pain or cough medicines combined with benzodiazepines: serious side effects. 2016.
